metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `VerifyConfig` dataclass and `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercion table in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as explicit edit targets for the `enforce_plan_material_pins` bool field, and verify the `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` fail-open catch handles attribute-absent (not just ConfigError) paths.
evidence: VerifyConfig dataclass (lines 211–327 of _config_schema.py) enumerates all verify.* keys; `enforce_plan_material_pins` is absent
_SECTIONS['verify'] dict (lines 571–588) is the exhaustive coercion table; `enforce_plan_material_pins` is absent
mcp_readonly() at config.py:402 reads through load_config() which routes through the typed schema — adding a new key requires schema registration to avoid the 'unknown key' warning/error path
Plan says 'modeled on mcp_readonly()' but mcp_readonly reads config.mcp.readonly — a field already registered in McpConfig and _SECTIONS['mcp']
findings.finding: The plan asserts `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` can be added via a `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` reader boundary without naming `VerifyConfig` dataclass and `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercion table as edit targets. The config schema at `_config_schema.py` uses a strictly-typed, enumerated dataclass + coercion table. Adding a new `verify.*` key requires explicit registration in both `VerifyConfig` (lines 211–327) and `_SECTIONS['verify']` (lines 571–588). Any config file that sets an unregistered key produces a `logger.warning` (default) or raises `ConfigError` (strict mode) BEFORE `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` can read it, breaking the claimed 'fail-open on ConfigError' pattern.
findings.location: Implementation section — '`_read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root) -> bool` … catches `ConfigError`'; also `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` VerifyConfig dataclass and `_SECTIONS['verify']` dict
scenarios: A project sets `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins = true` in rebar.toml; the config loader warns 'unknown key verify.enforce_plan_material_pins ignored' (or raises in strict mode); `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` gets `load_config()` but `.verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` doesn't exist as an attribute — AttributeError, not ConfigError, so the fail-open catch doesn't trigger
With strict=True (REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error), setting the key raises ConfigError at load_config() — before _read_enforce_plan_material_pins can catch it cleanly
findings.suggested_fix: Add `enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False` to `VerifyConfig` and `'enforce_plan_material_pins': lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` to `_SECTIONS['verify']`; then `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` can read `load_config().verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` and catch `ConfigError` as planned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (`VerifyConfig` dataclass field + `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercer entry) as a required edit target for the `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` key.
evidence: _config_schema.py VerifyConfig dataclass at line 211 defines all verify.* fields
_SECTIONS['verify'] dict at line 570-589 maps each key to its coercer — this is the single source of truth for valid verify keys
New verify keys that are not added to both VerifyConfig and _SECTIONS will raise ConfigError on any config file that sets them
Pattern confirmed by verify.overlap_enabled, verify.enable_code_review, verify.require_plan_review_for_claim etc all appearing in both VerifyConfig and _SECTIONS
findings.finding: The plan adds verify.enforce_plan_material_pins to the [verify] config section but does not name src/rebar/_config_schema.py (VerifyConfig dataclass + _SECTIONS['verify'] coercer dict) as a required edit target. Every other verify.* flag follows this exact pattern and requires both edits.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins with default false and strict enabled-mode validation'
scenarios: Implementer adds the config key read logic in _read_enforce_plan_material_pins but forgets to register it in _SECTIONS, causing ConfigError for any project that sets verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'Edit `_config_schema.py`: add `enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False` to `VerifyConfig` and add `"enforce_plan_material_pins": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` to `_SECTIONS["verify"]`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that 'migrate claim-gate and idempotent-reuse validity' means adding the `profile=` parameter to existing `compute_validity` call sites (both already call it), not introducing a new routing path.
evidence: _idempotent_reuse at __init__.py:438 calls claim_gate_check (line 454), which at attest.py:759 calls compute_validity
claim_gate_check at attest.py:728 directly calls compute_validity at line 759
The plan states 'Migrate claim-gate and idempotent-reuse validity to this function; child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close'
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `_idempotent_reuse`'s validity is being 'migrated' to `compute_validity`, but reading `__init__.py:438–486` confirms `_idempotent_reuse` ALREADY routes validity through `claim_gate_check` → `compute_validity`. The plan's claim that claim-gate and idempotent-reuse validity both need to be 'migrated to this function' is only partially true: `claim_gate_check` directly calls `compute_validity` (confirmed attest.py:759) and `_idempotent_reuse` calls `claim_gate_check`. The actual new work is passing the `profile` parameter — but the plan describes this as a 'migration' implying the current code bypasses `compute_validity`.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Migrate claim-gate and idempotent-reuse validity to this function'
scenarios: Implementer believes claim_gate_check and _idempotent_reuse need to be rewritten to call compute_validity for the first time, but they already do — the actual work is only adding the profile= parameter to the call chain
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'migrate' with 'extend existing call sites in claim_gate_check and _idempotent_reuse to pass profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT'.

findings.finding: The plan states 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture' for `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`. However, no `verify.*` boolean config key in the codebase has fail-open behavior — `load_config()` raises `ConfigError` on invalid/malformed config and explicitly does NOT cache errors ('A ConfigError is propagated and NOT cached'). The only fail-open config pattern in the codebase is `mcp_readonly()`, which is an explicit per-function try/except, not a system-wide posture. The 'matching existing gate posture' rationale is false: the existing gate posture for `verify.*` is fail-closed on config errors.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root) -> bool` helper that catches `ConfigError` and returns False with a warning log, modeled after `mcp_readonly()` — and cite this explicit pattern in the plan rather than the false 'existing gate posture' rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `pin_status` assigned by `derive_plan_material_pin_health` when a target exists but its current fingerprint cannot be read (IO/reducer failure distinct from deletion): either `stale-pin-missing` (fail-closed) or a documented alternative.
evidence: Plan: 'resolves current target states' — no exception/error handling protocol specified for the resolution step itself
Plan: 'Missing or deleted targets produce stale-pin-missing' (AC) — covers only the missing/deleted case, not IO failure on a present target
Plan: 'Malformed record syntax is data, not an exception at these consumer boundaries' — covers only the pin record parsing side, not the target fingerprint read side

Child acaf specifies `PlanRelationSnapshotError` for collection-time failures, but `derive_plan_material_pin_health` is a different helper invoked at validity-check time with no equivalent error taxonomy
findings.finding: `derive_plan_material_pin_health` 'resolves current target states' (reads current fingerprints for each pinned target), but the plan does not specify the fallback `pin_status` when a current fingerprint resolution fails due to an IO error or reducer failure that is NOT a deletion (i.e., the target exists but is transiently unreadable). The plan specifies `stale-pin-missing` for 'missing/deleted' targets and `malformed-pin` for bad syntax, but leaves a runtime-read-failure case unaddressed.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'derive_plan_material_pin_health' paragraph; AC 'Missing or deleted targets produce stale-pin-missing'
scenarios: Tracker is temporarily unreadable during `compute_validity` — a specific target's fingerprint cannot be computed; implementor must decide between `stale-pin-missing`, `malformed-pin`, or a new status
An enforcement-enabled deployment would either crash or silently produce wrong health for the transient failure case
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` paragraph: 'A target whose current fingerprint cannot be computed due to a read or reducer error (not merely absent/deleted) is treated as `stale-pin-missing` (fail-closed when enforcement is enabled).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `derive_plan_material_pin_health` catches all target-resolution I/O/OS errors and maps them to `stale-pin-missing` (or a new `resolution-error` status) rather than propagating exceptions, consistent with the existing malformed-syntax fail-safe policy.
evidence: 'resolves current target states' — plan specifies no error handling for the filesystem/repo access needed to compute current_fingerprint
'Malformed record syntax is data, not an exception at these consumer boundaries' — only covers parse errors, not I/O errors
Compare with `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` which explicitly catches ConfigError — no equivalent specified for target-resolution I/O
findings.finding: File I/O or resolution errors when `derive_plan_material_pin_health` fetches current target fingerprints (via `repo_root`) are not specified to be caught or degraded gracefully — any OS/filesystem error at this path would propagate as an unhandled exception into every validity-check consumer.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Add one shared `derive_plan_material_pin_health`...' and § 'resolves current target states'
scenarios: Repo root is temporarily unavailable (NFS mount, permission change) — every attestation validity check raises instead of returning a degraded health result
A pinned target's backing file has a permission error — `compute_validity` throws instead of returning `stale-pin-missing` or similar safe status
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the helper spec: 'Any I/O or resolution error while fetching a target's current fingerprint treats that target as `stale-pin-missing` and records a structured warning; it does not raise.' Mirror the `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` pattern.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add named pytest file paths in the Verification section for each currently-unnamed test category: drift-refresh (`test_drift_refresh_candidate.py` or similar), PlanValidityProfile isolation, structured-config-warning (`test_plan_material_pin_config.py` or similar), and completion-verifier shape preservation — one file per AC cluster.
evidence: Named pytest files are only: test_attestation_invalidation.py, test_plan_review_resign.py, test_plan_review_sidecar.py

Prose says 'Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, sidecar compatibility, structured-config-warning, and config-golden tests' but no file names are given for drift-refresh, config-golden, or structured-config-warning tests
ABSENCE: No AC maps to a test file that exercises drift_refresh_candidate or PlanValidityProfile isolation
findings.finding: Multiple ACs have no named proving test file or command: ACs covering `drift_refresh_candidate` delegation to `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)`, PlanValidityProfile isolation (CLOSE/DRIFT_REFRESH each disable only code freshness), completion-verifier return shape unchanged, and the structured config-warning emission are not mapped to any of the three named pytest files.
findings.location: Verification section; ACs: 'drift_refresh_candidate delegates…', 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH each disable only code freshness…', 'Existing plan-review validity keys retain their values with additive health, and completion-verifier return shape remains unchanged', 'Unreadable optional enforcement configuration returns false and emits the exact structured warning without raising'
scenarios: An executor writing tests has no named target file for drift_refresh_candidate delegation tests, may create ad-hoc files not covered by CI gating
Config-warning structured emission test is described only in prose; if omitted, the AC passes CI silently
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the pytest command to: `pytest -q tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py tests/unit/test_drift_refresh_candidate.py tests/unit/test_plan_material_pin_config.py` and name each file alongside the AC(s) it proves.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-ship audit step (e.g., grep/AST search for callers of `compute_validity` that do not use dict-key or keyword access) and enumerate any callers requiring migration before this story ships.
evidence: 'all in-repo callers must use named keys/tolerate extras' — stated as a requirement, not as a verified precondition
No mention of a grep/audit step, a migration for non-conforming callers, or a test that validates caller compatibility
Child cb6b and child 2bbd are named consumers but their tolerance is not confirmed
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'all in-repo callers must use named keys/tolerate extras' as a deploy-ordering precondition for the additive `health` key, but provides no verification step, audit, or fallback if a caller uses positional unpacking or strict schema validation — leaving a silent compatibility break.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Adding health to plan-review results is an additive internal interface change; all in-repo callers must use named keys/tolerate extras.'
scenarios: A caller that pattern-matches or destructures the compute_validity result strictly (e.g., `valid, reason, verdict = compute_validity(...)`) raises ValueError on the new health key
A serialization boundary that uses strict schema validation rejects the new field, breaking an unrelated feature

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific in-repo callers of `compute_validity` that consume plan-review results (not completion-verifier) and confirm each tolerates extra keys, rather than relying on the generic 'all in-repo callers' obligation.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/completion.py:176 calls compute_validity with kind='completion-verifier' — confirmed out-of-dir caller
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:653 calls compute_validity with kind='completion-verifier' in _attested_delivered
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:759 calls compute_validity with kind=plan-review (in drift_refresh_candidate / claim_gate_check area)
Plan says health is omitted for completion-verifier — consistent, so completion.py does not need updating
No exhaustive caller enumeration is provided in the plan
findings.finding: The plan's compute_validity return-shape change (adding a `health` key for plan-review kind) affects callers outside plan_review/ that consume compute_validity plan-review results. The plan states 'all in-repo callers must use named keys/tolerate extras' but does not enumerate them. The confirmed out-of-dir caller src/rebar/llm/completion.py:176 uses kind='completion-verifier' (explicitly excluded from health). However, _attested_delivered in attest.py:631 also calls compute_validity with kind='completion-verifier'. The plan enumerates neither the caller list nor which specific files need tolerate-extras validation.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Adding health to plan-review results is an additive internal interface change; all in-repo callers must use named keys/tolerate extras'
scenarios: A caller that destructures compute_validity results as a fixed tuple or uses exact key matching breaks when health is added; without enumeration the implementer may miss one

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the rollback section to explicitly cover restoring `drift_refresh_candidate`'s removed inline guards if `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)` delegation is found defective (e.g., note that the guards should be re-inlined or the profile call reverted).
evidence: 'Rollback removes the additive health key and its consumers; no stored-data migration is required.' — rollback section only covers the health key, silent on guard removal.
ABSENCE: no expand-contract sequence or restore-guards step is described for the drift_refresh_candidate refactor.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback statement covers only the additive `health` key removal but does not address rollback of the `drift_refresh_candidate` guard removal. Removing the inline signature, kind, registry, and own-material guards is a behavioral change delegated to `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)`; if that delegation has a defect, the back-out path requires restoring those guards without guidance.
findings.location: Implementation — 'In `drift_refresh_candidate`, call `compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DRIFT_REFRESH)` on the verified attestation result. Remove the redundant inline signature, kind, registry, and own-material guards'
scenarios: A bug in `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)` silently skips a guard (e.g., registry check) that the removed inline guard would have caught; the rollback procedure only describes removing the health key, leaving engineers without guidance on restoring the inline guards.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'If DRIFT_REFRESH delegation is found defective, rollback of drift_refresh_candidate restores the removed inline signature/kind/registry/own-material guards from version control; no data migration is needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` explicitly as a required edit target in the Implementation, describing which existing guards it retains and which new `derive_plan_material_pin_health` call it adds.
evidence: resign_plan_review lives in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py (confirmed by grep)
Plan says resign_plan_review reads reviewed_related_material and invokes derive_plan_material_pin_health on those records before signing
Plan says 'with enforcement enabled, stale drift, missing targets, or malformed records refuse re-sign'
Current resign.py only reads sidecar material_fingerprint and calls attest.sign_plan_review — no pin health logic
Plan names attest.py as the home of derive_plan_material_pin_health but does not name resign.py as an edit target
findings.finding: resign.py (containing resign_plan_review) is a required edit target not named anywhere in the Implementation. The plan specifies that resign_plan_review must read reviewed_related_material from sidecar records and invoke derive_plan_material_pin_health before signing, and must refuse re-sign when enforcement is enabled and pins are stale/malformed — but the file src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py is never mentioned as an edit target.
findings.location: Implementation — 'resign_plan_review does not call compute_validity...' bullet
scenarios: Implementer edits attest.py to add derive_plan_material_pin_health but forgets to wire it into resign.py, silently shipping a re-sign path that ignores pin drift even with enforcement enabled
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under Implementation: 'Edit `resign.py`: after the existing verdict/material staleness guards, read `reviewed_related_material` from the sidecar payload, call `derive_plan_material_pin_health(records, repo_root=repo_root, enforced=_read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root))`, and refuse re-sign when enforcement is enabled and health is not `current` or `legacy-unpinned`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sentence in the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` or `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` description specifying that multiple `TargetPinDetail` records sharing the same (role, canonical_id) pair are treated as malformed-pin input and produce a `malformed-pin` aggregate status.
evidence: AC text: 'Duplicate role-plus-ID pins produce malformed-pin'
Implementation: 'TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin}'
Implementation: 'Malformed record syntax is data, not an exception at these consumer boundaries'
ABSENCE: no sentence in Implementation body specifies duplicate detection as a triggering condition for malformed-pin
findings.finding: AC 15 ('Duplicate role-plus-ID pins produce malformed-pin') has no corresponding described implementation logic. The plan body defines malformed-pin as a status value and says 'Malformed record syntax is data, not an exception,' but nowhere describes duplicate role+ID detection as a defined trigger condition. An implementer reading only the Implementation section would not know this behavior is required.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item: 'Duplicate role-plus-ID pins produce malformed-pin'; Implementation section: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' and 'derive_plan_material_pin_health' paragraphs
scenarios: Implementer skips duplicate detection entirely because it is not mentioned in the body; AC 15 then fails at review time with no clear implementation guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' paragraph, add: 'A pin record set containing more than one entry with the same (role, canonical_id) pair is treated as structurally malformed; `derive_plan_material_pin_health` returns `malformed-pin` aggregate status for such input without raising.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md section or ADR capturing the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract design, `PlanValidityProfile` separation rationale, severity ordering policy, and the fail-open key-local rollout pattern so multi-child consumers have a durable reference.
evidence: ABSENCE: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
'This is an intentional key-local fail-open rollout policy modeled on `mcp_readonly()`' — a new reusable pattern without documentation.
'Child 5967 extends the same contract' and 'child cb6b owns audit and UI rendering calls' — multi-child contract with no documented decision record.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions — the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract as the single pin-validity interface across all attestation lifecycle consumers, the `PlanValidityProfile` enum with its deliberate `CLOSE`/`DRIFT_REFRESH` separation, and the fail-open key-local rollout policy modeled on `mcp_readonly()` — but does not mention updating AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design document.
findings.location: Implementation — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition, PlanValidityProfile, and _read_enforce_plan_material_pins fail-open policy sections
scenarios: Future contributors extending the contract (child 5967 phase_status, cb6b rendering) encounter conflicting interpretations of severity ordering or fail-open policy because the decisions are only embedded in a story plan rather than a durable design document.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured `logger.info` (or debug-level) emission from `derive_plan_material_pin_health` when advisory drift is detected, so operators have interim visibility before cb6b ships.
evidence: 'child cb6b owns audit and UI rendering calls'
'No warning event is persisted in the ticket event store for derived health'
No interim logger.info/warning or metric specified for advisory drift in this story's scope
findings.finding: Advisory pin-drift state has no operator-visible instrumentation until child cb6b lands; the plan explicitly defers all surfacing to that child with no interim metric, log line, or alert, leaving a deployment window where drift silently accumulates undetected.
findings.location: AC: 'No warning event is persisted in the ticket event store for derived health; advisory state is exposed through the returned health contract and child cb6b surfaces.'
scenarios: This story ships and cb6b is delayed — operators have no visibility into how many attestations are drifting in advisory mode
A misconfigured flag leaves enforcement off; drift grows silently with no signal until cb6b lands
findings.suggested_fix: In the helper, emit `logger.info('plan_material_pin_advisory_drift', pin_status=..., target_id=...)` for each drifted target when enforcement is disabled. This is additive and does not affect cb6b's eventual UI/audit work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the sidecar write in `resign_plan_review` (and the initial sidecar builder write) is atomic (write-to-temp-then-rename or equivalent) and that concurrent re-sign is safe (e.g., last-write-wins with a content hash guard, or an advisory lock).
evidence: 'resign_plan_review ... reads `reviewed_related_material`, and invokes `derive_plan_material_pin_health` on those records before signing' — check-then-act on external state
No mention of atomic write, file locking, or idempotency guard for the re-sign output
'The sidecar builder validates and preserves these' — no atomicity specified for the sidecar write itself
findings.finding: `resign_plan_review` performs a read-then-write sequence (read `reviewed_related_material` → call `derive_plan_material_pin_health` → conditionally sign) with no atomicity guarantee on the sidecar write; a crash or concurrent re-sign between the health check and the signing step could leave a partially written or inconsistently signed artifact.
findings.location: Implementation § '`resign_plan_review` does not call `compute_validity`...' and § 'sidecar builder validates and preserves these as `reviewed_related_material`'
scenarios: Two concurrent re-sign calls on the same ticket both pass the health check, both proceed to sign, resulting in two different signatures or a torn write
Process crashes after health check passes but before signing completes, leaving the sidecar in an inconsistent state

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend the plan title or add a 'Scope note' section acknowledging that `PlanValidityProfile` introduction and claim-gate/idempotent-reuse consumer migration are in-scope for this story, so implementers and reviewers have a complete picture of deliverables.
evidence: 'Define `PlanValidityProfile` with exactly `DEFAULT`, `CLOSE`, and `DRIFT_REFRESH`.'
'Migrate claim-gate and idempotent-reuse validity to this function; child 2bbd later wires `CLOSE` into `transition_close`'
Title: 'Derive plan-material-pin health and enforce optional drift invalidation' — no mention of validity-profile infrastructure or consumer migration
findings.finding: The plan introduces `PlanValidityProfile` (DEFAULT, CLOSE, DRIFT_REFRESH) and migrates claim-gate and idempotent-reuse validity consumers to `compute_validity` — work that is not reflected in the title 'Derive plan-material-pin health and enforce optional drift invalidation'. The CLOSE profile is defined here but its call-site wiring is deferred to child 2bbd, meaning this story delivers infrastructure for a goal it does not complete.
findings.location: Implementation § PlanValidityProfile / 'Migrate claim-gate and idempotent-reuse validity to this function'
scenarios: An implementer reading only the title scopes work to health derivation and enforcement, misses the profile enum and consumer migration, and delivers an incomplete story; the downstream child 2bbd then finds `PlanValidityProfile` absent and fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the title to 'Derive plan-material-pin health, enforce optional drift invalidation, and introduce PlanValidityProfile' or add a brief 'Also in scope' bullet at the top of Implementation listing the profile enum and the two consumer migrations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Motivation / Current Behavior section that names the concrete gap or defect in the status quo (e.g., 'currently, pins emitted at sign time are never re-validated at compute_validity, so a drifted target passes the claim gate') and, if possible, a minimal reproduction or Expected-vs-Actual.
evidence: The Goal reads only 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer, while preserving safe rollout for existing projects.' — this states intent, not a current defect.
No section names a bug, reproduces a failure, or shows an Expected-vs-Actual for current behavior.
Implementation jumps directly to 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins…' without explaining what currently happens when a pin drifts.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a substantial mechanism (health contract, enforcement flag, profile abstraction, sidecar extension, re-sign guards) but never states the current behavior that is wrong or insufficient. There is no reproduction, no Expected-vs-Actual, no named defect, and no gap statement explaining what problem the current attestation lifecycle exhibits today (e.g., 'pins are emitted by child acaf but never validated at claim time, allowing drifted attestations to be accepted as valid'). A reader cannot verify that the change is needed without understanding the status quo.
findings.location: Goal / Implementation sections (entire plan)
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm that the existing compute_validity does not already perform this check in some form.
Without a stated current behavior, scope creep is harder to detect — the plan may add checks the system already performs.
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a brief 'Current Behavior / Gap' subsection under Goal: state what the current `compute_validity` / `drift_refresh_candidate` / `resign_plan_review` code does (or fails to do) with signed pins today, and name the concrete consequence (e.g., stale attestations accepted, drifted targets not detected). One or two sentences suffice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` should be deferred to child 2bbd (define it there when needed) rather than shipped unused in this story; if kept here, add at least one AC verifying CLOSE is selectable and produces the expected profile behavior.
evidence: Plan text: 'child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close' — CLOSE has no current-story consumer.
grep confirms no existing PlanValidityProfile or CLOSE in src/rebar.
Rule-of-Three: a value that is defined but unused at ship is premature by definition; a future child story that needs it can add the value then.
findings.finding: `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` is defined in this story but its only wiring call-site is explicitly deferred to child 2bbd (`transition_close`). At this story's ship boundary CLOSE has zero active consumers, making it a premature abstraction (Rule-of-Three requires >=3 existing call sites; CLOSE has 0 at this story's scope).
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'Define PlanValidityProfile with exactly DEFAULT, CLOSE, and DRIFT_REFRESH… child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close'
scenarios: CLOSE ships unused in this story; if child 2bbd is descoped or redesigned, CLOSE becomes dead code with no test coverage.
A future policy change to CLOSE's behavior (e.g. re-enabling code freshness for close) must navigate the pre-existing enum value rather than simply adding it when needed.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the CLOSE value definition (and its AC) to child 2bbd, which owns the `transition_close` wiring. This story needs only DEFAULT and DRIFT_REFRESH.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC in this story that tests CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH produce visibly different behavior (e.g. CLOSE skips an additional check that DRIFT_REFRESH does not), or defer CLOSE to child 2bbd when the distinction actually matters.
evidence: Plan quote: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story' — explicit acknowledgment of identical present behavior.
YAGNI criterion: the differentiation is justified by a future scenario, not a current AC or done-definition in this story.
No AC verifies that CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH produce different results in any test in this story.
findings.finding: `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` and `PlanValidityProfile.DRIFT_REFRESH` are described as having **identical behavior** in this story ('disable only code freshness'), yet are introduced as separate enum values. The only stated justification is anticipatory: 'so later close policy cannot silently change progressive refresh.' This is a YAGNI pattern — the differentiation serves a hypothetical future divergence, not a current done-definition.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story—disable only code freshness—but remain distinct call-site intents so later close policy cannot silently change progressive refresh.'
scenarios: Both values produce identical output at this story's scope; tests cannot distinguish them, meaning the 'distinct intent' guarantee is untestable until child 2bbd.
If child 2bbd redesigns the close profile to also skip a different check, the story author must remember this pre-committed enum split and its undocumented invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that child cb6b (or another consumer in this story) directly calls `derive_plan_material_pin_health`, bringing the call-site count to >=3; if not, consider inlining the logic into its two call-sites and extracting the helper only when the third consumer materializes.
evidence: Named consumer 1: 'compute_validity invokes it after signature and plan-review-kind verification.'
Named consumer 2: 'The re-sign recovery invokes it on sidecar records as described below.'

No third named call-site in this story; child cb6b is named as a rendering consumer but the plan states child cb6b 'surfaces' the health contract, not that it calls derive_plan_material_pin_health directly.
Rule-of-Three: 2 call-sites at story scope.
findings.finding: `derive_plan_material_pin_health` is proposed as a shared helper but has exactly **2 named call-sites** in this story (`compute_validity` and `resign_plan_review`). The Rule-of-Three threshold for extracting a shared helper is >=3 actual call-sites; with 2 sites the abstraction may be premature.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 3: 'Add one shared derive_plan_material_pin_health(pin_records, *, repo_root, enforced) helper in attest.py. … compute_validity invokes it … The re-sign recovery invokes it on sidecar records.'
scenarios: If resign_plan_review is restructured in a future story, the helper may become single-use with no abstraction benefit.
The two call sites differ in their input type (signed-manifest pins vs sidecar records) — the helper must handle both, adding generality that may not be warranted at 2 sites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit implementation step describing how `compute_validity` conditionally omits `health` for `kind='completion-verifier'` (e.g., only populate `health` in the `plan-review` branch at attest.py:543).
evidence: compute_validity at attest.py:519-541 handles kind='completion-verifier' and returns {valid, reason, verdict} — no health key
completion.py:176 calls compute_validity(sig, c, 'completion-verifier', repo_root=repo_root) and uses .get('valid')
The plan asserts the completion-verifier branch will 'omit health' — but adding health conditionally requires a code branch not described in the Implementation
findings.finding: The plan asserts `compute_validity` currently returns `{valid, reason, verdict}` and will have `health` added as a named key. Reading `attest.py:471–627` confirms the current return shape is exactly `{valid, reason, verdict}`. The plan describes this as 'an additive internal interface change; all in-repo callers must use named keys/tolerate extras.' However, at `rebar/llm/completion.py:176` `compute_validity` is called outside `plan_review/` for `kind='completion-verifier'` and its result is used via `.get('valid')`. The plan explicitly states 'completion-verifier results retain their existing shape and omit health' — this exemption is asserted but not verified by any greppable implementation detail. There is no existing mechanism in `compute_validity` to return different shapes per kind (the current code returns plain dicts). This requires the implementer to add kind-conditional return shaping, which is not described as a code change.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Completion-verifier results retain their existing shape and omit health'; `src/rebar/llm/completion.py:176`
scenarios: Implementer adds health to all compute_validity returns without a kind guard; completion.py callers receive unexpected health key (tolerable with named access, but the plan explicitly promises omission)
Implementer adds a kind guard to omit health for completion-verifier but forgets to update the AC that checks the completion-verifier return shape stays unchanged
findings.suggested_fix: State that `health` is only set in the `if kind == _MANIFEST_PREFIX:` branch (attest.py:543), so the `completion-verifier` early-return at line 537 naturally omits it — making the shape-omission a consequence of existing code structure rather than a new conditional.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hallucinated `tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py` (and confirm `tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py`) in the Verification pytest command with the actual new test file names that will be created, or note they are new files to be authored.
evidence: Search for 'test_plan_review_sidecar' returned no source-file hits, only an unrelated audit coaching test
tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py also has no search hits — potentially a second hallucinated file
tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py DOES exist (confirmed by multiple grep hits)
findings.finding: The Verification section's pytest command references `tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py`, but this file does not exist in the repository. It appears to be a hallucinated test module name.
findings.location: Verification — 'Run pytest -q tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py'
scenarios: CI runs the specified pytest command verbatim and fails immediately because the file doesn't exist, preventing automated verification of ACs 4 and 7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the fixed severity-ordering aggregation rule (`malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current`) into the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper specification (or add it as an explicit note on the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` schema block) so it is co-located with the code that must enforce it.
evidence: Plan states: 'Aggregate target status by fixed severity malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current' — in the contract paragraph
The `derive_plan_material_pin_health` paragraph omits the aggregation rule entirely
TargetPinDetail schema definition contains no reference to how its per-entry `pin_status` rolls up to the parent `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status`
Neither `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` nor any existing file defines this contract today — it is entirely new and must be implemented from the plan text alone
findings.finding: The aggregation rule that reduces `targets: list[TargetPinDetail]` into the single top-level `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` (fixed severity ordering: malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current) is stated in a prose paragraph of the Implementation section, not co-located with the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` schema definition or the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper specification. An implementor defining the struct will not have the decision rule at hand when writing the aggregation logic.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' paragraph and 'derive_plan_material_pin_health' paragraph
scenarios: Implementor writes `derive_plan_material_pin_health` and must search the plan document to find the aggregation rule rather than reading it alongside the schema
A future editor changes the severity ordering in the contract paragraph without updating the helper spec (or vice versa), creating a split definition
findings.suggested_fix: In the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` paragraph, add: 'The returned `pin_status` is the maximum-severity status across all `targets` using the fixed ordering: malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current; legacy-unpinned is returned only when `targets` is empty and the manifest carried no pin records.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `phase_status: Literal['compatible', 'incompatible', 'malformed'] | None` (default `None`) as a stub field on `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` in this plan's schema definition, with a note that child 5967 populates it, so the struct layout is fully specified in one place and both stories can be implemented independently.
evidence: Plan under review: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with `phase_status` and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format'
Child 5967 (ticket 5967-4476-7f9f-442d) AC: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed under the documented planning/execution comparison'
`DerivedPlanReviewHealth` struct as defined in this plan has no `phase_status` field at all — it is listed as a future extension
Child cb6b (rendering story) depends on both this story and 5967, suggesting it consumes a combined contract not fully defined in either story alone
findings.finding: `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` is defined in this plan as the owned contract, but its `phase_status` field — referenced as 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with `phase_status` and phase compatibility' — has its full value vocabulary (`compatible`, `incompatible`, `malformed`) defined only in child 5967's ticket, not in this plan. The struct layout returned by `derive_plan_material_pin_health` (which this plan defines) therefore cannot be finalized by an implementor reading only this plan.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' paragraph; child 5967 ticket AC: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed'
scenarios: Implementor of this story defines `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` as a dataclass/TypedDict; child 5967 then must add a field to a type defined in a different story, creating a coordination hazard
Rendering consumers in child cb6b must know the combined shape before both parent stories ship

findings.finding: `resign_plan_review` operates on SIDECAR data (from `sidecar.latest_review_result()`), not a verified attestation record; but `compute_validity` requires a `signing.verify_signature()` result dict as its first argument. The plan asserts 'resign_plan_review replaces its inline material/staleness checks with compute_validity(DEFAULT) before signing' — but those inline checks compare `payload.get('material_fingerprint')` (a sidecar field) against the current fingerprint. Passing a sidecar dict to `compute_validity` would either fail at the `isinstance(attestation, dict)` guard or produce incorrect results because the sidecar lacks `verified`, `manifest`, `signed_at`, and other attestation fields that `compute_validity` reads. The 'replaces' claim is architecturally imprecise.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'resign_plan_review replaces its inline material/staleness checks with compute_validity(DEFAULT) before signing'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that resign_plan_review 'replaces its inline material/staleness checks with compute_validity(DEFAULT) before signing,' but compute_validity requires a certified attestation mapping as its first argument — an object resign_plan_review cannot supply in its primary use case (recovery from a failed sign where no certified attestation exists). The resign flow reads a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, not a verify_signature result, so passing it to compute_validity would immediately return {valid: False, verdict: 'unsigned'}, permanently blocking re-sign.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'resign_plan_review replaces its inline material/staleness checks with compute_validity(DEFAULT) before signing' / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py

findings.finding: The plan says 'resign_plan_review replaces its inline material/staleness checks with compute_validity(DEFAULT),' but compute_validity's defined contract is 'Per-kind lifecycle/freshness validity for an ALREADY-CERTIFIED attestation record' — its first argument is an attestation mapping from verify_signature. resign_plan_review's entire use case is recovery when signing failed, so there is no prior certified attestation to pass. The plan does not explain how compute_validity(DEFAULT) is invoked in this context or what attestation object is constructed to pass in.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'resign_plan_review replaces its inline material/staleness checks with compute_validity(DEFAULT) before signing'; resign.py lines 35-148

findings.finding: The plan asserts 're-sign' as one of the consumers migrated to compute_validity with the profile parameter, but resign_plan_review (resign.py:35-148) currently performs its own staleness check (recorded_material vs current_material at line 101) and calls attest.sign_plan_review directly, without ever calling compute_validity.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Implementation or Verification section whether child acaf is a prerequisite to this story (and thus emission is already present), or whether ACs 12-13 are deferred regression tests that only activate after child acaf merges; alternatively, reframe ACs 12-13 as 'enforcement configuration does not alter the emission code path introduced by child acaf' with an explicit ordering note.
evidence: AC 12: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled'
AC 13: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled'
Implementation: 'Normal sign_plan_review still emits the complete current pin set as owned by child acaf'
No story ordering or prerequisite statement clarifies whether child acaf must complete before this story
findings.finding: ACs 12 and 13 verify that `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true/false` 'leaves new pin emission enabled,' but the plan body explicitly delegates pin emission to child acaf ('Normal `sign_plan_review` still emits the complete current pin set as owned by child acaf'). It is ambiguous whether this story can verify emission behavior at all — if child acaf has not yet run, emission does not exist in this story's scope, making the criteria potentially unmeasurable within this story's test suite.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria lines: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and '=false leaves new pin emission enabled'; Implementation section: 'Normal sign_plan_review still emits the complete current pin set as owned by child acaf'
scenarios: If child acaf has not yet introduced pin emission, the test suite for ACs 12/13 cannot exercise or assert emission behavior, making these criteria vacuously pass or fail depending on test infrastructure assumptions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` and `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json` to the file_impact list and specify the `reviewed_related_material` field's sub-schema (type, required/optional, record shape) in the Implementation section alongside the sidecar extension description.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records … the sidecar builder validates and preserves these as `reviewed_related_material`'
Plan file_impact lists: `attest.py`, `resign.py`, and test files — but NOT `sidecar.py` or `plan_review_verdict.schema.json`

Existing `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` defines `emit()`, `build_payload()`, and the JSON schema reference; `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json` defines the REVIEW_RESULT schema
Child acaf (ticket acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c) file_impact also does not list `sidecar.py` or the schema file for this extension
findings.finding: The plan adds a `reviewed_related_material` field to the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload ('Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` records'), but neither `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` nor `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json` appears in this plan's file_impact list. The schema extension — the decision rule for how the new field is validated and preserved — is not co-located with the existing sidecar schema file.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload' paragraph; plan file_impact list
scenarios: Implementor extends sidecar payload but does not update the JSON schema validator — schema validation rejects the new field on older readers
The `reviewed_related_material` field's required/optional status and its record sub-schema (role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint) are not covered by schema tests

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a positive rationale for why `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` must be fully computed (including O(N_targets) current-fingerprint reads) before existing fast-fail early returns rather than lazily after existing checks pass or on caller demand, and confirm this is consistent with the ~50ms claim-gate target.
evidence: Plan: 'After signature certification and plan-review-kind validation, parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns; every such plan-review return includes health.'
attest.py module docstring (line 13-16): 'The claim path is a FAST, LOCAL check only — no LLM, no network beyond a couple of local reads — so it stays well within the ~50ms target.'
TargetPinDetail requires resolving 'current_fingerprint: string|null' — each a live ticket read via current_material_fingerprint() — for every pinned target, before the reopen check fires.
ABSENCE: plan provides no rationale for why health must be computed unconditionally before existing fast-fail guards rather than lazily after they pass.
findings.finding: The plan places `derive_plan_material_pin_health` (which performs O(N_targets) live ticket reads to resolve current fingerprints) before ALL early returns in `compute_validity`, including the reopen check that fires immediately for reopened attestations. The existing module docstring explicitly documents a ~50ms target for the claim path. Every attestation that fails a fast early return (stale-reopened, stale-regver) now pays the full O(targets) read cost even though health is not actionable when a prior check already fails-closed. The plan states the ordering as a requirement but provides no positive rationale for why pre-check evaluation is worth the latency cost over a lazy/post-check alternative.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns; every such plan-review return includes health'
scenarios: A reopened ticket's claim gate call now performs O(children + prerequisites) ticket reads before returning stale-reopened — the fastest existing failure path is degraded proportionally to the pin count.
A stale-regver attestation similarly pays the full O(targets) read cost before the existing registry check fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) move health computation after the existing fast-fail guards so that the O(N) reads only occur when the attestation is otherwise valid, attaching `health=None` or `health={'pin_status':'legacy-unpinned'}` to fast-fail returns, or (b) explicitly state in the plan that callers requiring health on fast-fail returns are non-claim-path callers and provide the latency bound argument for the claim path specifically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify whether `reviewed_related_material` is added to the existing `plan_review_result_v2` schema sentinel (requiring no reader-guard update) or a new sentinel is introduced (requiring an atomic reader-guard update in the same change), and add an AC that the reader returns the correct sidecar in a mixed-version store during partial rollout.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records already collected before the LLM by child acaf. The sidecar builder validates and preserves these as reviewed_related_material'
Existing reader hardcoded guard: 'plan_review_result_v1' and 'plan_review_result_v2' only (sidecar.py:149–153)
Plan does not state whether the schema sentinel changes or stays at v2
resign_plan_review reads the sidecar to extract reviewed_related_material; schema guard determines which files are returned
findings.finding: The plan adds `reviewed_related_material` to REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payloads but does not specify whether the schema sentinel remains `plan_review_result_v2` or bumps to a new version. The existing `latest_review_result` (and `all_review_results`) reader schema guard is hardcoded to `{plan_review_result_v1, plan_review_result_v2}` — if a schema version bump is introduced, a partial deployment window exists where new sidecars (bumped schema) are written but the reader guard has not yet been updated, causing the re-sign path to fall back to an older pre-feature sidecar and silently skip `reviewed_related_material` rather than reading the freshest data. Conversely, if the sentinel stays at `v2`, that must be explicitly asserted as by-design to confirm the reader requires no update.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records'; also sidecar.py latest_review_result schema guard (lines 149–153)
scenarios: Partial deployment: new sidecar emitted with bumped schema (v3) before reader guard is updated — resign_plan_review walks back to an older v2 sidecar lacking reviewed_related_material, silently omitting the field and treating it as legacy-unpinned even though a post-feature sidecar is present.

Rollback: if schema was bumped then rolled back, the rolled-back reader cannot consume any new-format sidecars emitted between deploy and rollback, causing resign_plan_review to fall through to legacy-unpinned incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: State in the Implementation section: 'The `reviewed_related_material` field is added to the existing `plan_review_result_v2` payload without a schema version bump; the absence of the field on older v2 sidecars is the legacy-unpinned signal, already covered by the absent-record-set path.' This removes the ambiguity and confirms no reader-guard change is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default/sentinel for `pin_records` in `derive_plan_material_pin_health`: specify that both `None` and an empty list are treated as `legacy-unpinned` (or pick one and document it).
evidence: 'treats an absent record set as `legacy-unpinned`' — 'absent' is not defined as None vs [] vs omitted
The sidecar compatibility AC says 'pre-feature sidecars behave as legacy-unpinned' but the helper's call-site contract for that case is not specified
findings.finding: The `pin_records` parameter type for `derive_plan_material_pin_health` does not specify whether 'absent' means `None`, an empty list, or a missing key, leaving the boundary condition for `legacy-unpinned` ambiguous.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add one shared `derive_plan_material_pin_health(pin_records, *, repo_root, enforced)` helper… treats an absent record set as `legacy-unpinned`'
scenarios: An executor might pass `[]` for an empty manifest and get legacy-unpinned, while another passes `None` and gets a TypeError, or vice versa — behavior diverges across consumers
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the helper description: 'Callers pass `None` when the sidecar or manifest has no pin records field; an empty list `[]` is treated identically to `None` — both produce `legacy-unpinned`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
